author: Wang, Kuang-Hsien
title: From fear to adaptation: The dynamic impact of AI on worker behavior and technological well-being
abstract_note: The rapid rise of generative AI, marked by ChatGPT's launch, has reshaped the labor market by intensifying technological substitution and prompting workers to adjust their behavior and mindset. This study applies a Difference-in-Differences model to examine how AI substitution concerns have evolved in Taiwan's labor market. Using national survey data from 2020 (before ChatGPT), 2022 (its launch year), and 2023 (one year after), it explores differences across skill levels and industries, offering a broad view of AI's labor market impact. The findings show that workers most concerned about AI replacement initially increased their use of digital skills during ChatGPT's adoption phase, but this adjustment weakened over time. Their technological well-being followed a pattern of short-term decline, mid-term recovery, and long-term divergence. Service sector workers, more reliant on digital tools, adjusted faster and adapted more effectively than manufacturing workers, who exhibited slower, steadier changes. Skill-level analysis reveals that high-skill workers quickly adapted to technological demands and saw improvements in well-being. In contrast, low-skill workers struggled significantly, highlighting the need for urgent skill retraining. This study fills a critical gap by adopting a dynamic approach to examine skill differentiation, industry disparities, and psychological adaptation to AI. It recommends that policymakers focus on nationwide skill retraining and psychological support programs to help workers adapt. These measures are essential for balancing technological progress, labor market stability, and workforce development.

automatic_tags: Artificial intelligence; Behavioral adaptation; ChatGPT; Difference-in-Differences; Subjective technological well-being; Technological disruption
